quiz_questions.question_text: Which value is most likely an outlier in the data set {12, 14, 15, 13, 16, 14, 50}?
quiz_questions.explanation: Most values cluster between 12 and 16, but 50 is far from the rest, making it an outlier.

quiz_questions.question_text: Adding an outlier to a data set will most affect which measure of center?

options.text: Median
Mode
Mean
None of the above

quiz_questions.explanation: The mean uses every value in its calculation, so an extreme outlier will pull the mean significantly toward it.

quiz_questions.question_text: The data set {20, 22, 24, 25, 23} has a mean of 22.8. If 100 is added, the new mean becomes approximately:
quiz_questions.explanation: New sum = 20 + 22 + 24 + 25 + 23 + 100 = 214. New mean = 214 / 6 ≈ 35.7. The outlier raised the mean from 22.8 to about 35.7.

quiz_questions.question_text: Using the 1.5 x IQR rule, a data set has Q1 = 10 and Q3 = 20. Which value would be an outlier?
quiz_questions.explanation: IQR = 20 - 10 = 10. Upper fence = 20 + 1.5(10) = 35. Lower fence = 10 - 1.5(10) = -5. Since 36 > 35, it is an outlier.

quiz_questions.question_text: Does an outlier significantly affect the median of a large data set?

options.text: Yes, the median always changes dramatically
No, the median is resistant to outliers
Only if the outlier is negative
Only if there are multiple outliers

quiz_questions.explanation: The median is resistant to outliers because it depends only on the middle value(s), not on the magnitude of extreme values.

quiz_questions.question_text: How does a high outlier affect the range of a data set?

options.text: It decreases the range
It increases the range
It has no effect on the range
It doubles the range

quiz_questions.explanation: A high outlier becomes the new maximum, increasing the difference between the max and min, which increases the range.

quiz_questions.question_text: Data set: {5, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12}. The mean is about 8.86. If the value 5 is removed, what happens to the mean?

options.text: It stays the same
It increases
It decreases
Cannot determine

quiz_questions.explanation: Removing the lowest value (5) increases the mean. New sum = 57, new count = 6, new mean = 57/6 = 9.5, up from 8.86.

quiz_questions.question_text: A data set has a mean of 50 and a median of 50. After adding an extremely high value, the mean becomes 58 and the median becomes 51. Which statistic changed more?

options.text: The median
They changed equally
The mean
Neither changed

quiz_questions.explanation: The mean increased by 8 (from 50 to 58) while the median increased by only 1 (from 50 to 51). The mean changed much more because it is not resistant to outliers.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which measure of spread is most affected by outliers?

options.text: IQR
Range
Median
Mode

quiz_questions.explanation: The range is calculated using the max and min, so a single outlier directly changes the range. The IQR is resistant because it only uses Q1 and Q3.

quiz_questions.question_text: A data set with no outliers has a range of 15. A value of 200 is added. What is the most likely effect?

options.text: The range stays 15
The range increases significantly
The range decreases
The range becomes 200

quiz_questions.explanation: Adding 200 (likely much higher than the current max) makes it the new max, dramatically increasing the range.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an 'outlier'?

options.text: A data value that is significantly higher or lower than the rest of the data
The median of a data set
The most common value in a data set
Any value above the mean

quiz_questions.explanation: An outlier is a data value that lies far away from the rest of the data, either much higher or much lower than the majority of values.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the '1.5 x IQR rule' mean?

options.text: A method for identifying outliers by checking if a value falls more than 1.5 times the IQR above Q3 or below Q1
A method for calculating the mean
A way to find the median
A formula for standard deviation

quiz_questions.explanation: The 1.5 x IQR rule identifies outliers as values below Q1 - 1.5(IQR) or above Q3 + 1.5(IQR), creating 'fences' beyond which values are considered outliers.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'resistant' mean when describing a statistic?

options.text: The statistic does not change much when outliers are added or removed
The statistic always equals zero
The statistic cannot be calculated
The statistic increases with every new data point

quiz_questions.explanation: A resistant statistic is one that is not significantly affected by extreme values or outliers. The median and IQR are resistant; the mean and range are not.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'upper fence' mean in the context of outliers?

options.text: The value Q3 + 1.5 x IQR, above which data points are considered outliers
The maximum value in the data set
The mean plus the standard deviation
The third quartile

quiz_questions.explanation: The upper fence is calculated as Q3 + 1.5 x IQR. Any data value above this fence is classified as a potential upper outlier.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'lower fence' mean in the context of outliers?

options.text: The minimum value in the data set
The value Q1 - 1.5 x IQR, below which data points are considered outliers
The first quartile
The mean minus the standard deviation

quiz_questions.explanation: The lower fence is Q1 - 1.5 x IQR. Any data value below this fence is classified as a potential lower outlier.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'pulling the mean' mean when discussing outliers?

options.text: An outlier shifts the mean toward the outlier's value
An outlier removes the mean from the data set
The mean becomes the outlier
The mean is unchanged by outliers

quiz_questions.explanation: When an outlier is present, it 'pulls' the mean toward its value—a high outlier increases the mean and a low outlier decreases it.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'skew' mean in relation to outliers?

options.text: Outliers can create or increase the skewness of a distribution by adding a long tail
Outliers always make a distribution symmetric
Skew and outliers are unrelated
Skew only occurs when there are no outliers

quiz_questions.explanation: Outliers can create or increase skewness in a distribution by stretching one tail, making it longer than the other.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'non-resistant' mean for a measure like the mean?

options.text: The measure is strongly influenced by extreme values
The measure cannot be calculated
The measure is always zero
The measure ignores all data points

quiz_questions.explanation: A non-resistant measure is significantly affected by outliers or extreme values. The mean and range are non-resistant because they use every value.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'extreme value' mean in statistics?

options.text: The mean of the data set
A data value that is unusually large or small compared to the rest of the data
The mode of the data set
A value equal to zero

quiz_questions.explanation: An extreme value is one that is much larger or smaller than the majority of data values and may qualify as an outlier.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does it mean to 'identify outliers'?

options.text: Using a rule (like the 1.5 x IQR rule) to determine which data values are unusually far from the rest
Removing all data values from the set
Calculating the mean of the data
Finding the mode of the data

quiz_questions.explanation: Identifying outliers means using a systematic method (often the 1.5 x IQR rule) to determine which values are far enough from the bulk of the data to be considered outliers.

quiz_questions.question_text: A teacher recorded test scores for 10 students: 78, 82, 80, 85, 79, 83, 81, 84, 80, 35. The mean without the score of 35 is 81.3. What is the mean with 35 included, and how much did the outlier change it?

options.text: 76.7; decreased the mean by 4.6 points
80.0; decreased the mean by 1.3 points
81.3; no change
73.2; decreased the mean by 8.1 points

quiz_questions.explanation: Sum with 35: 78+82+80+85+79+83+81+84+80+35 = 767. Mean = 767/10 = 76.7. The outlier decreased the mean from 81.3 to 76.7, a drop of 4.6 points.

quiz_questions.question_text: A real estate agent listed 8 home prices in a neighborhood (in thousands): $180, $195, $200, $210, $205, $190, $185, $750. A buyer asks for the 'average' home price. Should the agent use the mean or median, and why?

options.text: Mean, because it includes all prices
Median, because the $750K home is an outlier that inflates the mean
Mode, because it shows the most common price
Range, because it shows the price spread

quiz_questions.explanation: The $750K home is a clear outlier. The median ($197.50) better represents a typical home price than the mean ($264.375K), which is inflated by the outlier.

quiz_questions.question_text: A factory measures widget weights in grams: 50.1, 50.3, 49.8, 50.0, 50.2, 49.9, 50.1, 55.0. Quality control needs to determine if 55.0g is an outlier using the 1.5 x IQR rule. Is it an outlier?

options.text: No, because it is close to the other values
Yes, because it exceeds the upper fence
No, because it is within the fences
Cannot be determined

quiz_questions.question_text: A company's employee salaries (in thousands) are: $40, $42, $45, $43, $41, $44, $120. The CEO asks what effect removing the $120K salary has on the range. What happens?

options.text: Range decreases from $80K to $5K
Range stays at $80K
Range increases
Range decreases from $80K to $40K

quiz_questions.explanation: With the outlier: range = 120 - 40 = $80K. Without: range = 45 - 40 = $5K. Removing the outlier dramatically reduces the range.

quiz_questions.question_text: A weather station recorded daily high temperatures for a week: 72°F, 75°F, 73°F, 74°F, 71°F, 76°F, 45°F (a cold front). How does the outlier of 45°F affect the median compared to the mean?

options.text: The mean drops more than the median
The median drops more than the mean
Both are equally affected
Neither is affected

quiz_questions.explanation: Ordered: {45, 71, 72, 73, 74, 75, 76}. Median = 73 (barely changed from what it would be without 45). Mean = 486/7 ≈ 69.4 (dropped significantly). The mean is much more affected.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student scored 95, 88, 92, 90, 91 on five tests. She accidentally entered a 9 instead of 90 for one test in her spreadsheet, making the data {95, 88, 92, 9, 91}. How does this error affect the mean?

options.text: The mean drops from 91.2 to 75
The mean stays at 91.2
The mean increases to 95
The mean drops from 91.2 to 88

quiz_questions.explanation: Correct mean: (95+88+92+90+91)/5 = 456/5 = 91.2. Error mean: (95+88+92+9+91)/5 = 375/5 = 75. The data entry error (creating an outlier of 9) dropped the mean by 16.2 points.

quiz_questions.question_text: A doctor measures the resting heart rates of 9 patients: 68, 72, 70, 75, 74, 71, 73, 69, 120. The patient with 120 bpm had just finished exercising. How should the doctor handle this outlier when reporting typical resting heart rates?

options.text: Include 120 and use the mean
Use the median or exclude the outlier and explain why
Replace 120 with the mean of other values
Report 120 as the typical heart rate

quiz_questions.explanation: Since 120 bpm was due to exercise (not a true resting rate), the doctor should either use the median (which is resistant) or exclude the outlier with an explanation.

quiz_questions.question_text: A store tracked daily customers: 85, 90, 88, 92, 87, 91, 250 (holiday sale). The manager wants to predict normal daily traffic. What is the best approach?

options.text: Use the mean of all 7 days (111.9)
Use the median (90) or the mean without the holiday (88.8) to represent normal traffic
Use the maximum (250)
There is no good approach

quiz_questions.explanation: The holiday sale created an outlier (250). For normal daily traffic, the median (90) or the mean excluding the outlier (88.8) gives a better prediction.

quiz_questions.question_text: A data set of exam scores has Q1 = 65, Q3 = 85, and IQR = 20. A student scored 32. Using the 1.5 x IQR rule, is 32 an outlier?

options.text: No, because 32 > 0
Yes, because 32 < 35 (the lower fence)
No, because 32 is within the fences
Cannot determine without the full data set

quiz_questions.explanation: Lower fence = Q1 - 1.5(IQR) = 65 - 1.5(20) = 65 - 30 = 35. Since 32 < 35, the score of 32 is below the lower fence and is an outlier.

quiz_questions.question_text: A researcher collects response times (in seconds) for an experiment: 1.2, 1.4, 1.3, 1.5, 1.1, 1.4, 1.3, 8.5. The value 8.5 occurred because the participant was distracted. If the researcher reports both the mean (2.21s) and the median (1.35s), what should readers conclude?

options.text: The mean and median are both equally good summaries
The large gap between mean and median suggests an outlier is pulling the mean up
The median is too low to be accurate
The data set has no outliers

quiz_questions.explanation: The mean (2.21s) is much higher than the median (1.35s) because the outlier of 8.5s pulls the mean up. This large gap signals the presence of an outlier.
